examples.title: Binary Tree Node Definition and Basic Creation
examples.description: Demonstrates the fundamental definition of a binary tree node and how to create a simple binary tree manually, illustrating the basic structure where each node has at most two children (left and right).
examples.code:
class TreeNode:
    """Definition of a binary tree node"""
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.left = None  # Reference to left child
        self.right = None  # Reference to right child

# Creating a simple binary tree
#         10
#        /  \
#       5    15
#      / \
#     3   7

root = TreeNode(10)
root.left = TreeNode(5)
root.right = TreeNode(15)
root.left.left = TreeNode(3)
root.left.right = TreeNode(7)

# Traversing to verify structure
def inorder(node):
    if node:
        inorder(node.left)
        print(node.data, end=' ')
        inorder(node.right)

print("Inorder traversal:")
inorder(root)
examples.explanation: A binary tree is defined as a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and right child. In this example, we define a TreeNode class with three attributes: data (stores the value), left (pointer to left child), and right (pointer to right child). We then manually construct a sample tree with root value 10, demonstrating how nodes are connected using references.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is height of tree

examples.title: Computing Binary Tree Properties (Height, Size, Count)
examples.description: Demonstrates key properties of binary trees - height (maximum depth), size (total number of nodes), and leaf count - fundamental properties used to analyze tree algorithms.
examples.explanation: This example illustrates three fundamental properties of binary trees: (1) Height - the length of the longest path from root to a leaf, where an empty tree has height -1 and a single node has height 0; (2) Size - the total count of all nodes in the tree; (3) Leaf count - the number of nodes that have no children. The functions use recursive traversal (depth-first) to compute each property, visiting every node exactly once.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(h) due to recursion stack, where h is height

examples.title: Binary Tree Type Properties (Full, Complete, Perfect)
examples.description: Demonstrates advanced binary tree property checking - determining if a binary tree is full (every node has 0 or 2 children), complete (all levels filled except possibly last), or perfect (all internal nodes have 2 children and all leaves are at same level).
examples.code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

def is_full_binary_tree(node):
    """Property: Full tree - every node has either 0 or 2 children"""
    if node is None:
        return True
    # Leaf node is valid
    if node.left is None and node.right is None:
        return True
    # Node must have both children to be full
    if node.left is None or node.right is None:
        return False
    return is_full_binary_tree(node.left) and is_full_binary_tree(node.right)

def is_complete_binary_tree(node, index=0, total_nodes=None):
    """Property: Complete tree - all levels filled except possibly last"""
    if node is None:
        return True
    if total_nodes is None:
        total_nodes = count_nodes(node)
    if index >= total_nodes:
        return False
    return (is_complete_binary_tree(node.left, 2*index+1, total_nodes) and
            is_complete_binary_tree(node.right, 2*index+2, total_nodes))

def count_nodes(node):
    if node is None:
        return 0
    return 1 + count_nodes(node.left) + count_nodes(node.right)

def is_perfect_binary_tree(node, depth=0, level=None):
    """Property: Perfect tree - all internal nodes have 2 children, all leaves at same level"""
    if node is None:
        return True
    if node.left is None and node.right is None:
        if level is None:
            level = depth
        return depth == level
    if node.left is None or node.right is None:
        return False
    return (is_perfect_binary_tree(node.left, depth+1, level) and
            is_perfect_binary_tree(node.right, depth+1, level))

# Test with different trees
# Tree 1: Perfect Binary Tree
#         1
#        / \
#       2   3
#      / \ / \
#     4  5 6  7

perfect_tree = TreeNode(1)
perfect_tree.left = TreeNode(2)
perfect_tree.right = TreeNode(3)
perfect_tree.left.left = TreeNode(4)
perfect_tree.left.right = TreeNode(5)
perfect_tree.right.left = TreeNode(6)
perfect_tree.right.right = TreeNode(7)

# Tree 2: Complete but not full
#         1
#        / \
#       2   3
#      /
#     4

complete_tree = TreeNode(1)
complete_tree.left = TreeNode(2)
complete_tree.right = TreeNode(3)
complete_tree.left.left = TreeNode(4)

# Tree 3: Full but not complete
#         1
#        / \
#       2   3
#      / \
#     4   5

full_tree = TreeNode(1)
full_tree.left = TreeNode(2)
full_tree.right = TreeNode(3)
full_tree.left.left = TreeNode(4)
full_tree.left.right = TreeNode(5)

print("Perfect Tree:")
print(f"  Full: {is_full_binary_tree(perfect_tree)}")
print(f"  Complete: {is_complete_binary_tree(perfect_tree)}")
print(f"  Perfect: {is_perfect_binary_tree(perfect_tree)}")

print("\nComplete Tree:")
print(f"  Full: {is_full_binary_tree(complete_tree)}")
print(f"  Complete: {is_complete_binary_tree(complete_tree)}")
print(f"  Perfect: {is_perfect_binary_tree(complete_tree)}")

print("\nFull Tree:")
print(f"  Full: {is_full_binary_tree(full_tree)}")
print(f"  Complete: {is_complete_binary_tree(full_tree)}")
print(f"  Perfect: {is_perfect_binary_tree(full_tree)}")
examples.explanation: This example demonstrates three important binary tree classifications based on structural properties: (1) Full Binary Tree - every node has either 0 or exactly 2 children, no node has exactly 1 child; (2) Complete Binary Tree - all levels are completely filled except possibly the last level, which is filled from left to right (used in heap implementation); (3) Perfect Binary Tree - all internal nodes have exactly 2 children and all leaf nodes are at the same level (has 2^h+1 - 1 nodes where h is height). The code tests each property with different tree configurations to illustrate the differences.
